exemplar.title: To what extent does insecure attachment in early childhood affect the risk of developing obesity later?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does insecure attachment in early childhood affect the risk of developing obesity later?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and effectively at the outset and revisited throughout.
Research question is sharply focused and consistently linked to each section of the essay.
Use of WHO statistics grounds the investigation in real-world data.
weaknesses: Minor drift into discussion of under-weight outcomes slightly dilutes the central focus.
Methodology lacks explicit explanation of search strategy and inclusion/exclusion criteria.
Future recommendations do not fully address gaps in paternal and alternate caregiver attachment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic and purpose with clarity (band 5-6) and formulates a sharply delimited research question (band 5-6). Methodology is appropriate and well described but lacks transparency in source selection (band 4). Overall performance sits securely in the 5-6 band but with a partial methodology shortcoming, warranting a final score of 5.

strengths: Accurate summary of central concepts in attachment theory and obesity research with reference to key empirical studies.
Mostly correct and consistent use of subject-specific terminology (e.g., BMI percentile, longitudinal design).
weaknesses: Reliance on non-academic sources (Verywell Mind, ChatGPT) for foundational theory undermines scholarly depth.
Occasional inaccuracies in terminology (e.g., âbasal metabolic indexâ) and minor statistical slips.
Some definitions and concepts lifted verbatim rather than rephrased in studentâs own words.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and understanding are solid (band 3-4): the student demonstrates clear command of theory and correct use of terminology, but occasional inaccuracies and dependence on popular sources prevent a top-band performance. A final score of 4 reflects this upper-band but not excellent standard.

strengths: Good research quality with multiple longitudinal studies and relevant epidemiological data.
Effective analysis in parts, such as evaluation of observer bias and cross-cultural sampling.
Critical evaluation of methodological limitations (e.g., parent-report bias, small sample sizes).
weaknesses: Analysis often remains descriptive, with limited synthesis or theoretical integration.
Discussion lacks a clearly signposted line of reasoning, making evaluation somewhat repetitive.
Statistical reporting omits confidence intervals and p-values, reducing analytical rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance falls into the 4-6 band for all three strands: research is good and mostly relevant, analysis is clear but not deeply probed, and evaluation is partially critical. The combination of strengths and noted gaps justifies a conservative mid-band score of 4 out of 12.

strengths: Overall structure follows IB conventions (title page, TOC, introduction, body, conclusion, bibliography).
Table of contents is clearly presented and aids navigation.
weaknesses: Formatting is inconsistent: misspelt headers (âBiobliographyâ), incomplete URLs, uneven spacing.
Citation style is not uniform and several references are split across lines.
Uneven page numbering and abrupt page breaks disrupt flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets the basic requirements (band 3-4) but formatting and layout contain multiple oversights (band 1-2). On balance, the presentation warrants a score of 2, reflecting an otherwise clear organization tempered by inconsistent application of layout conventions.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological terminologyâsecure/insecure attachment, anxious-avoidant, BMI percentile, longitudinal study, confirmation biasâappears regularly and is applied correctly.  Definitions are mostly accurate and integrated within analysis.  A few lapses (âbasal metabolic indexâ instead of body mass index) and occasional stylistic inconsistencies prevent a fully consistent top-band performance, so the work best fits the high end of the 3-4 band.

Required layout elements are mostly present: word count, title, and (apparently) page numbers.  Nonetheless, formatting is inconsistent (misspelt âBiobliographyâ, variable spacing, citation style not uniform) and several references are incomplete URLs split across lines.  These weaknesses indicate that some layout considerations have been overlooked, fitting the 1-2 band rather than the fully correct 3-4 band.

The student accurately summarises central concepts in attachment theory (secure vs. insecure, sub-types) and obesity research, referencing Bowlby, Ainsworth, Anderson & Whitaker, etc.  Empirical findings are generally reported correctly with pertinent statistics (e.g., obesity prevalence percentages, odds ratios).  Occasional inaccuracies (mis-spelling of authors, minor statistical slips) and reliance on explanatory websites keep the demonstration at a good rather than excellent level, warranting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The methodology is a literature-based secondary analysis that draws on several peer-reviewed longitudinal studies, supported by WHO statistics and recognised attachment measures (e.g., AQS, Strange Situation).  Source selection is mostly relevant and scholarly; however, some popular websites (Verywell Mind, Simply Psychology) are mixed with academic sources, and the search/selection process is not explained.  The essay does not outline criteria for inclusion or discuss alternative search strategies, so evidence of disciplined, informed selection is partial rather than comprehensive, placing it solidly in the 3-4 band.

The student states the topic (âChildhood attachment style and obesityâ) and the precise research question at the very start, then reiterates the purpose in the introduction and conclusion.  The rationale for the investigation (the rising prevalence of childhood obesity and the possible role of early attachment) is explicitly explained, showing why the topic is worthy of study.  Throughout the essay each major section (Exploration, Discussion, Conclusion) is explicitly tied back to the guiding question.  Because the topic, purpose, and focus are communicated both accurately and effectively, the work fits the top-band descriptor.

The student cites around eight primary empirical studies and relevant epidemiological data; almost all directly address the relationship between attachment and weight status, demonstrating clear relevance.  Coverage spans different age points and countries, though it is still a relatively narrow pool for an EE and includes some non-scholarly sources.  Overall research quality and relevance are good but not exemplary, meriting a solid mid-band score (4-6).

The research question â âTo what extent does insecure attachment in early childhood affect the risk of developing obesity later?â â is clearly stated, sharply delimited (specific population, time-frame, and variable relationship), and is consistently referenced in analysis and evaluation.  Minor drift occurs when the student briefly discusses under-weight outcomes, so the focus is not perfectly sustained, but overall the link between the RQ and the discussion remains strong.  Hence a secure placement in the 5-6 band, but not the very top of that range.

The essay is organised according to IB conventions (title page, table of contents, introduction, exploration, discussion, conclusion, bibliography).  Headings and sub-headings follow a logical sequence and help navigation.  Minor issuesâuneven page numbering in the extract, occasional abrupt page breaksâprevent a perfect score but the overall structure is clearly appropriate, positioning the work at the lower end of the top band (3-4).

The discussion section identifies methodological limitations (observer bias, parent self-report), cultural considerations, and alternative explanations, showing evaluative thinking.  However, the argument structure is somewhat repetitive and lacks a clearly sign-posted line of reasoning that leads step-by-step to the final conclusion.  Evaluation remains partially critical rather than fully critical, hence a mid-band placement.

Analysis moves beyond description by comparing methodologies (e.g., observational bias in AQS, strengths of longitudinal design) and considering confounding variables.  Nevertheless, much space is devoted to summarising study procedures and statistical results with limited synthesis or theoretical integration, and some claims are left undeveloped (e.g., brief mention of cortisol without elaboration).  The conclusions drawn are generally supported but not deeply probed, aligning with the lower half of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The essay does not explain how sources were selected or search criteria; include selection strategy to strengthen methodology transparency.
The title and research question are communicated clearly and effectively at the start, establishing precise purpose and focus for the investigation.
Including the precise word count demonstrates adherence to the IB wordâlimit requirement.
Table of contents is clearly presented and follows conventional layout, aiding navigation through the essay.
Typographical error in âBiobliographyâ in the table of contents; correct spelling to maintain professionalism.
Heavy reliance on a nonâacademic source (Verywell Mind) for foundational theory; integrate primary texts by Bowlby and Ainsworth for stronger subject knowledge.
Consider rephrasing definitions of key terms in your own words and citing original research to demonstrate deeper understanding.
Inline citation â(MSEd, 2023)â indicates a popular website source; seek peerâreviewed studies to support theoretical background.
Sentence is cut off midâcitation â(Jan, 2024)â; ensure complete references to maintain clarity and accuracy.
Use of WHO statistics on obesity trends effectively grounds the investigation in realâworld data.
Link statistical trends explicitly back to the research question to reinforce the focus of the investigation.
Methodology description of Anderson & Whitaker (2011) is clear and appropriate for a secondaryâdata analysis.
Conclusion does not fully address research gaps in paternal and alternative caregiver attachment; expand future research recommendations.
How were inclusion and exclusion criteria determined when selecting studies? Add a brief subsection outlining this process.
Statistical findings are reported without confidence intervals or pâvalues; include these to enhance analytical rigor.
Justify the quartile threshold used to define insecure attachment; explain why lower quartile was chosen.
Term âbasal metabolic indexâ is incorrect; use âbody mass index (BMI)â consistently.
Critical reflection on observer bias in the AQS method demonstrates sound evaluative thinking.
Cite empirical evidence on the magnitude of observer bias effects to support your evaluation.
Inline math â~2 hoursâ should use proper LaTeX: $$2\,\text{hours}$$ for consistency in notation.
Including the Adult Attachment Projective (AAP) study broadens the scope of analysis by considering both parentsâ attachment.
Sample size in Mazzeschi et al. (n=44) is small; discuss implications for external validity and statistical power.
Integrate underweight findings alongside obesity studies to provide a cohesive synthesis rather than listing separately.
Consider how poverty may confound underweight outcomes in the Chilean study; discuss socioeconomic variables more critically.
Highlighting crossâcultural sampling strengthens research quality by demonstrating relevance across contexts.
Discussion omits reflection on paternal attachment in earlier studies; address this gap to deepen evaluation.
Repetition of bias limitations across paragraphs reduces conciseness; consolidate similar points for clarity.
Evaluation of longitudinal designs is strong, highlighting both causal inference and practical challenges.
Quantify participant attrition rates in longitudinal studies to illustrate the scale of missingâdata issues.
Bibliography shows inconsistent citation formatting and URLs split across lines; align with APA or IB style consistently.
Referencing ChatGPT as a source may undermine academic credibility; reconsider its inclusion or justify its role.
Page numbering appears inconsistent in footer placement; standardize style and location across all pages.
